coredata.dc:title: Reductive carbon dots for reduction, ratiometric fluorescence determination, and intracellular imaging of Au3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Karami, Sara
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Barati, Ali
Mohammadi, Soheila
Abedi Kichi, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: Many studies show that ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD) produces an oxidized fluorescent product when exposed to an oxidizing agent that enables the direct or indirect fluorescence detection of a range chemical and biochemical analytes. However, there is no report on this unique optical behavior for other two isomers of phenylenediamine. This study demonstrates that a simple hydrothermal treatment of para-phenylenediamine (PPD) in the presence of sulfuric acid results in the formation of fluorescent N, S-doped carbon dots (CDs) with triple functionalities including the reduction of Au3+ into gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), the stabilization of the produced AuNPs, and the determination of Au3+ concentration through an intrinsic ratiometric fluorescence signal. In the presence of Au3+, the blue emission of CDs at 437 nm quenched, and a green emission at 540 nm emerged. The linear concentration range for the determination of Au3+ was 20 nM–16 µM with a detection limit of 16 nM. Additionally, the dual emissive CDs-AuNPs hybrid probe showed potential for the indirect fluorescence ratiometric determination of cysteine and sulfide ions. The linear concentration range for cysteine and sulfide ions were 0.25–8 μM and 0.1–6 μΜ, with detection limits of 0.095 μM and 0.041 μM, respectively. Accordingly, CDs were applied to detect Au3+ and S2− in real water samples. Moreover, the synthesized CDs showed no cytotoxicity for HeLa cells up to 300 µg mL−1, as determined by the MTT assay. Therefore, their potential for intracellular imaging of Au3+ in living cells was also investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric sensing
Au3+ sensing
Gold nanoparticles
Bioimaging
Para-phenylenediamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fluorescence measurements of Au3+ ions by CDs
The fluorescence detection of Cys, and S2− by CDs-AuNPs system
Fluorescence response of CDs to Au3+
Fluorescence response of dual-emissive CDs-AuNPs system to Cys, and S2

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs, (b) XPS spectrum of CDs and deconvoluted C1s peak, (c) FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs-AuNPs system, and (d) The absorption and emission spectra of the synthesized CDs at pH 7.0 under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs, Au3+, and CDs + Au3+. Insets show the photographs of Au3+ solution, CDs solution, and CDs-AuNPs system under visible light, (b) TEM image of CDs-AuNPs system, (c) XPS spectra of CDs in the presence of Au3+ and deconvoluted C1s and Au 4f peaks, (d) Fluorescence response of the CDs under adding concentrations of Au3+ (0, 0.02, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 12, 15, 30, 50, and 65 µM. (e) Plot of ratiometric fluorescence responses (F540/F437) of the CDs versus Au3+ concentration. (f) Plot of ratiometric fluorescence responses (F540/F0 437) of the CDs versus Au3+ concentration. The linear stern–volmer calibration curve was inserted. (g) The response of the blue emission of CDs against 20 µM of some metal ions at pH 7.0, (h) Ratiometric fluorescence behavior of CDs (F540/F0 437) against 20 µM of some metal ions at pH 7.0, (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a, c) The fluorescence response of CDs-AuNPs upon increasing concentrations of Cys from 0 to 50 µM ([Cys]: 0, 0. 1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 25, and 50 µM.), and S2− from 0 to 65 µM ([S2−]: 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 20, 40, and 65 µM) at pH 7 and under excitation 340 nm. (b, d) Plot of ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F540/F437) of the CDs-AuNPs system against Cys and S2− concentration. The linear stern–volmer calibration curves were inserted.
Cell viability in the presence of different levels of (a) CDs, and (b) Au3+ on the HeLa cell line.

(b,e) Fluorescence microscopy images, (a,d) their corresponding bright field, and (c,f) merged images of HeLa cells after incubated with (a,b,c) 200 μg mL−1 CDs for 3 h at 37 °C, (d,e,f) 200 μg mL−1 CDs for 3 h at 37 °C and then 10 µM Au3+ for 1 h at 37 °C.
Performance comparison of various fluorescence nanoprobes based on CDs for the detection of Au3+.

row.entry: The non-radiative electron transfer from CDs to Au3+
The IFE between Au3+ and CDs
The reduction of Au3+ to Au NPs and the subsequent occurrence of IFE

head.ce:title: Reductive carbon dots for reduction, ratiometric fluorescence determination, and intracellular imaging of Au3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Cardiovascular Prevention (IPEK), Ludwig-Maximillians University, 80336 Munich, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many studies show that ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD) produces an oxidized fluorescent product when exposed to an oxidizing agent that enables the direct or indirect fluorescence detection of a range chemical and biochemical analytes. However, there is no report on this unique optical behavior for other two isomers of phenylenediamine. This study demonstrates that a simple hydrothermal treatment of para-phenylenediamine (PPD) in the presence of sulfuric acid results in the formation of fluorescent N, S-doped carbon dots (CDs) with triple functionalities including the reduction of Au3+ into gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), the stabilization of the produced AuNPs, and the determination of Au3+ concentration through an intrinsic ratiometric fluorescence signal. In the presence of Au3+, the blue emission of CDs at 437 nm quenched, and a green emission at 540 nm emerged. The linear concentration range for the determination of Au3+ was 20 nM–16 µM with a detection limit of 16 nM. Additionally, the dual emissive CDs-AuNPs hybrid probe showed potential for the indirect fluorescence ratiometric determination of cysteine and sulfide ions. The linear concentration range for cysteine and sulfide ions were 0.25–8 μM and 0.1–6 μΜ, with detection limits of 0.095 μM and 0.041 μM, respectively. Accordingly, CDs were applied to detect Au3+ and S2− in real water samples. Moreover, the synthesized CDs showed no cytotoxicity for HeLa cells up to 300 µg mL−1, as determined by the MTT assay. Therefore, their potential for intracellular imaging of Au3+ in living cells was also investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence methods are among the most interesting and rapidly developing analytical detection techniques, exhibiting enough simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, and short response times for detecting various chemical and biochemical analytes [1]. However, the accuracy of the single intensity-based fluorescence sensing method can be affected and weakened by several unavoidable analyte-independent interfering factors. Therefore, more attention has been paid to developing ratiometric fluorescence sensing methods that provide a self-calibrated response signal for more precise and accurate quantitative analyses [2].

Since their introduction as fluorescent carbonaceous nanoparticles by Xu et al. in 2004 [3], carbon dots (CDs) have found widespread applications in various fields, including sensing, bioimaging, biology, catalysis, and energy conversion [4]. Photophysical and chemical properties of CDs which make them more efficient in specific applications, are mostly defined by several factors, such as initial material source, heteroatom doping, surface functionalization, and synthesis condition [5]. For example, CDs have been reported as alternative reducing agents for the synthesis of metal nanoparticles [6], in which CDs act as effective electron donors to reduce metal ions and provide stable and dispersed solutions by covalently attaching to the surface of formed nanoparticles. The type of initial material source used for CDs synthesis is an essential factor in defining their electron-donating or electron-accepting properties.

Until now, various CDs synthesized from different carbon sources have been used for the detection of Au3+ [7–9]. For example, Sharma et al [7] used Rosa indica to synthesis nitrogen and sulfur co-doped rose CDs with interesting fluorescence turn “off-on-off” response towards S2− and Au3+. Ramanan and coworkers [8], synthesized highly luminescent CDs from waste expanded polystyrene. The selective and sensitive detection of Au3+ ions was demonstrated using these CDs as fluorescence probes, and coordination-induced aggregation was suggested as the observed fluorescence quenching mechanism. Liao et al [9] reported a simple and green strategy to produce water-soluble nitrogen-doped CDs via the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of the mixture of natural peach gum polysaccharide and ethylenediamine. The resulting CDs exhibited a fast and highly sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching effect toward Au3+ ions with the detection limit reached to 6.4 × 10−8 M. There are also a number of reports on the sensing of cysteine [10–13] and sulfide [14–16] by fluorescent CDs. However, in all cases the utilized CDs are used as a fluorescence probe with a single responding emission peak.

Moreover, it has been widely reported that ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD) can be easily oxidized by various types of oxidants to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox) as the green-emitting oxidized product [17,18]. The fluorescence signal of this product has been used to develop fluorescent sensors for the direct determination of chemical analytes with oxidative activity, such as Hg2+ [19], Cu2+ [20], Ag+ [21], Ce4+ [22], and H2O2 [23]. It has been also used for the indirect determination of chemical/biochemical analytes that can interfere with the oxidation process of OPD with oxidants like glutathione [24], glucose [25], uric acid [26], ascorbic acid [27], and histidine [28]. However, the oxidation process of OPD produces a single-intensity-based fluorescence probe with a green emission at about 560 nm. The other two isomers of phenylenediamine do not display this unique optical behavior, and therefore, their direct application for fluorescence measurements is not reported.

Herein, a facile strategy was developed to synthesis fluorescent N, S-doped CDs prepared from para-phenylendiamine (PPD) that reduce Au3+ into Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) and provide a unique ratiometric fluorescence response for sensitive determination of Au3+ ions. The synthesized CDs showed an excitation-independent emission behavior with a fluorescence emission peak at 437 nm. Upon the addition of Au3+, this emission peak was quenched and a new emission peak at 540 nm emerged. The hybrid probe of dual emissive CDs-AuNPs can be also applied for selective detection of sulfide and cysteine ions through competitive interaction with CDs to attach onto the surface of AuNPs. These CDs-AuNPs hybrid system provides the ability of ratiometric sensing of these analytes. In addition, along with the determination of Au3+ and S2− ions in real samples, the cytotoxicity of the prepared CDs for living cells and their potential in intracellular imaging were also examined.

Para-Phenylenediamine (PPD), L-cysteine (Cys), and all L-amino acids were purchased from Sigma- Aldrich. H2SO4 (98 %), Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), H2O2 (30 % (v/v)), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), KMnO4, Na2S·9H2O, and other routine reagents were bought from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp source. The excitation and emission slit widths were adjusted to 5 nm. All fluorescence measurements were achieved in a 1 cm quartz cell. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by an Agilent 8453 Diode Array Spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were studied using a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer over the range of 500–4000 cm−1. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were provided using a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV (K.N. Toosi University of Technology, Tehran, Iran). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) was studied by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope 8025-BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system. The cellular images were taken using a 3 Cell Cytation™ Imaging Multi-Mode Reader (BioTek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA). MTT assay was performed by a microplate reader (ELx800, Biotek, USA) at 570 nm, and pH measurements were made using a Metrohm pH Meter 713.

CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.03 g of PPD was dissolved in 8 mL H2SO4/H2O solution (5:3 v/v) to make a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. The obtained yellowish-brown product was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min and passed through a 0.2 μm membrane filter to remove large particles, and then it was dialyzed with a membrane bag (Mw = 1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the unreacted precursors. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried and its stock solution was stored in the refrigerator for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence measurements of Au3+ ions by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, 100 µL of the stock CDs solution was added to 3 ml of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.0. The various concentrations of Au3+ were spiked to the CDs solution to have final concentrations of 0.02–65 µM. The resulting mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 20 min, and then their fluorescence data were recorded under excitation at 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence detection of Cys, and S2− by CDs-AuNPs system
ce:section.ce:para: In the Cys, and S2− sensing processes, different concentrations of Cys (0.1–50 μM), or S2− (0.25–65 µM) were introduced into 3.0 ml of the CDs-AuNPs probe (the CDs solution after the addition of 20 μM Au3+) and the mixture was stirred for 6 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded upon excitation at 340 nm.

The synthesized CDs system was employed for the selective detection of Au3+ and S2− in mineral and tap water samples. The water samples were first filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membranes to remove any precipitates and then diluted with the same volume of PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.0). For Au3+ detection, 100 µL of CD stock solution was added to 3 ml of buffered samples. These samples were then spiked with the desired concentrations of Au3+ and their emission spectra were recorded. For S2− sensing, 100 µL of the CD stock solution and a final concentration of 20 µM Au3+ were added to 3 ml of the buffered samples. After complete mixing of the solution (20 min), the desired volumes of S2− from its stock solution were spiked and emission spectra were recorded at the same conditions. The concentration of all samples was determined by the standard addition method (n = 5).

For cell toxicity assays, HeLa cells (cervical cancer cell line) were modified in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). Cell cultures were maintained in humid conditions at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 and grown to 80 % confluency. The cells were then transferred to sterile 96-well plates at a density of 10,000 cells/well and 3 wells per treatment. The next day, the medium was changed and the cells were treated with various concentrations of CDs (i.e. 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, and 500 µg mL−1) and Au3+ ion (0, 10, 20, 40, 50, 80, 90, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 µM) and incubated for another 24 h. The viability of the cells was studied by MTT assay, 10 μL MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. Finally, 100 μL of DMSO was added to solubilize the formazan crystals formed, and the plates were read directly at 570 nm on a microplate reader.

HeLa cells were first grown in DMEM medium in 48-well plates overnight in a water-saturated atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C. A solution of CDs (200 µg mL−1) was then added to the medium culture of cells (3 h incubation). The supernatant was then carefully removed, followed by five repeated wash cycles with PBS buffer (10 mmol/L, pH 7.4) to remove free CDs from the cell culture medium. Finally, cell imaging was performed using a 3 Cell Cytation™ Imaging Multi-Mode Reader. To evaluate Au3+ uptake, cells were incubated with 200 µg mL−1 CDs for 3 h, and then with 10 µM Au3+ at 37 °C for a further 1 h.

TEM, FT-IR, UV–vis, and XPS were used to perform a comparative study on the surface groups of CDs before and after the reaction with Au3+. The TEM image of CDs showed monodispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 5.6 ± 2.4 nm (Fig. 1 a) [29]. The XPS full scan spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1b) presented five peaks at around 170, 234, 288, 400, and 537 eV, which were related to S2s, S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [30]. Deconvolution of the C1s peak reveals four main carbon peaks at 284.5, 285.7, 287.8, and 288.5 eV assigned to CC/CC, CN/CO/CS, CO/CN, and C(O)O, respectively [31]. Based on the FTIR spectra presented in Fig. 1c, the CDs exhibit several typical bands related to the OH/NH groups (3070–3758 cm−1), CH stretching vibrations (2900 cm−1), a sharp peak at 1640 cm−1 that can be related to the CO band of carbonyl and amide groups and CC stretching vibrations of aromatic rings, CN (1401 cm−1) bonds, and CS/CN/CO stretching vibrations (1110 cm−1) [32].

The optical properties of CDs are shown in Fig. 1d. The CDs solution presented an excitation-independent emission behavior with a maximum emission at 437 nm. The UV–vis spectrum of CDs shows a strong band at 240 nm due to the π-π* and n-π* transition of CC groups and a band at 312 nm assigned to the n-π* transitions of CN/CO groups [33].

The fluorescence response of the prepared CDs to various metal ions and different anions was checked. It was observed that the blue emission peak of CDs is significantly quenched and a new green emission peak emerged at 540 nm in the presence of Au3+. Fig. 2 a displays the UV–vis spectrum of the CDs after the addition of HAuCl4. It is widely recognized that the HAuCl4 solution shows a strong peak at 220 nm, along with a shoulder around 290 nm (Fig. 2a) which are assigned to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) bands of [AuCl4]− ions between Au and Cl ligands [34]. The UV–vis spectrum of the CDs after the addition of Au3+ showed a new broadband at 418 nm (extended to 600 nm) but none of the peaks related to the CDs at 312 nm and the HAuCl4 solution at 220 nm were detected. This effective decrease of LMCT of Au3+ ions is due to their reduction into the metallic state [33], which was also evident from the change in the visual color of the CDs solution from colorless to wine red (inset of Fig. 2a), a characteristic of Au NPs solution. The FTIR spectra of CDs after the addition of HAuCl4 exhibited several similar bands to those of CDs. However, some significant changes were observed when compared with bare CDs. The intensity of the OH/NH stretching bands at 3400 cm−1 and CO/CN stretching bands at 1100 cm−1 are weakened in Fig. 1c. In addition, the intensity of the sharp peak at 1620 cm−1 related to the CC/CO/CN vibrations bands is increased, and a new peak at 1554 cm−1 appears, which can be assigned to the formation of azo (NN) groups. These results suggest that the reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs leads to the oxidation of the surface groups of CDs.

Moreover, the XPS full scan spectrum and deconvoluted peaks of the CDs-AuNPs hybrid reveal an increase in the content of oxygen-containing groups in the C1s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2c) [35]. Specially, the intensity of the peak related to the C-O binding energy decreases, while the intensity of the peak assigned to the C(O)O binding energy increases [29,36]. A sharp decrease in the S2p and S2s peaks of CDs is detected, which can be related to the interaction between the surface sulfur-containing groups and Au NPs. Two new peaks located at 84.8 and 88.2 eV related to the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 orbitals respectively appeared, suggesting the formation of metallic Au (0) after the addition of HAuCl4 into the CDs solution [37–39]. Consistent with these results, the TEM image of CDs after the addition of Au3+ shows AuNPs with sizes ranging from 20 to 30 nm (Fig. 2b) [40,41]. CDs containing different functional groups can interact with the formed AuNPs through electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonds, and Van der Waals forces [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to Au3+
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs showed an excitation-independent emission behavior showing a blue emission color with maximum intensity at 437 nm when excited at 340 nm. Upon the addition of Au3+ a significant quenching was observed for this emission peak and a new peak at 540 nm emerged. The highest variation in the fluorescence intensity ratio of CDs with Au3+ was observed at pH 7.0 (Fig. S1). This can be due to the protonation of surface groups of CDs at lower pH values, as well as the production of hydroxide form of Au3+ at higher pH values, which in both cases the possibility of the interaction between CDs and Au3+ ions decrease. To evaluate the detection performance, the fluorescence response of CDs was recorded in the presence of increasing concentrations of Au3+ ranging from 20 nM to 65 µM, Fig. 2d. The results indicate that Au3+ led to a regular quenching in the blue emission intensity of the CDs, while a new green emission peak gradually emerged. The ratiometric fluorescence response (F540/F437) can be used for Au3+ sensing with a satisfactory linear relationship in the concentration range of 20 nM–16 µM (see Fig. 2e). The detection limit of the method was obtained at 16 nM based on a 3σ /k where σ is the standard deviation of the probe in the absence of Au3+, and k is the slope of the linear equation.

To study the selectivity of the probe, a similar procedure was carried out in the presence of 20 µM of various metal ions including Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Co3+, Cu2+, and Cd2+. The results showed that the presence of these metal ions only affects the blue emission peak of CDs (Fig. 2g), and none of them even at higher concentrations up to 100 µM initiates the green emission peak (Fig. 2h). It was found that Hg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, and Cd2+ ions had the highest interfering effects on the blue emission peak, respectively. Further investigations revealed that the green fluorescence response can be also employed for Au3+ sensing, as there was a linear relationship between its intensity and Au3+ concentration from 20 nM to 10 µM, with a detection limit of 45 nM (see Fig. 2f). This makes the green emission peak a selective signal for the measurement of Au3+ without interfering form other co-existing metal ions. Table 1 shows the analytical performance of the suggested nanoprobe for the detection of Au3+ that is compared with previously reported fluorescence nanoprobes. This table reveals that this is the first fluorescence ratiometric CD probe for Au3+, and in most cases, better or comparable analytical performances have been obtained compared to the previous reports.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of dual-emissive CDs-AuNPs system to Cys, and S2
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that the addition of Cys and S2− to the CDs solution containing Au3+ (CDs-AuNPs hybrid nanoprobe) restored the blue emission intensity with no considerable effect on the green emission intensity. This finding suggests that the fluorescence response of the hybrid nanoprobe to Cys and S2− can be used for their fast ratiometric sensing. Thus, the concentration of Au3+ was optimized to achieve the highest sensitivity and the lowest detection limit of Cys and S2− that indicated 20 μM of Au3+ as the optimal concentration for both purposes. The experimental parameters, including pH (ranging from 3.0 to 9.0) and incubation time, were also optimized. The maximum fluorescence response was achieved at pH 7.0 (Fig. S1), and no significant variation in the fluorescence intensity was observed after 6 min.

The quantification performance of the hybrid probe was investigated by recording the fluorescence response of the CDs-AuNPs hybrid probe upon increasing concentrations of Cys and S2− (Fig. 3a,c). As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensity of the hybrid probe at 437 nm is slowly restored by increasing the concentrations of Cys from 0.1 to 50 μM, and with S2− from 0.25 to 65 μM. The green emission of the hybrid nanoprobe at 540 nm remained unchanged since these anions could not affect the oxidized surfaces of CDs. The ratiometric fluorescence responses were linear to Cys concentration ranging from 0.1 to 6 μΜ, and to S2− concentration ranging from 0.25 to 8 μM (Fig. 5b, d). The limit of detections for Cys and S2− were 0.041 μΜ, and 0.095 μΜ, respectively. This detection limit for S2− is much lower than the maximum level of this ion (15 μM) permitted by the World Health Organization for drinking water [43]. The selectivity of the suggested nanoprobe was also checked by comparing its response to S2− with that of various anions (SCN−, I−, CN−, Cl−,
NO
2
-
, Br−, and F−) and to Cys with that of other amino acids (Glu, Gly, Tyr, Ala, Thr, Ser, Met, Leu). The results showed that these potential interfering species had a negligible effect on the fluorescence signal (Fig. S2). These results indicated that a dual emissive CDs-AuNPs hybrid probe can be also employed as a ratiometric sensor in the sensitive detection of Cys and S2−. The analytical performance of reported fluorescent nanoprobes based on CDs for the detection of Cys and sulfide are summarized in Table S3.

To determine the mechanism behind the fluorescence response of CDs to Au3+ ions, a series of additional experiments were performed. Firstly, the fluorescence response of CDs was examined in the presence of pre-prepared AuNPs. As shown in Fig. S3, the blue emission of CDs was significantly quenched by AuNPs, but no green emission peak was observed in the final spectrum. Moreover, the quenched fluorescence of CDs was restored by the addition of sulfur-containing species such as cysteine and sulfide that show high binding affinity for the surface of AuNPs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is not due to either the inner filter effect or the autocatalytic oxidation effect of AuNPs on CDs.

The blue emission quenching can be due to the surface adsorption of CDs on the AuNPs and subsequent energy transfer from the adsorbed CDs to the AuNPs as non-fluorescent acceptors through the common FRET mechanism [44,45]. The fluorescence intensity is restored when CDs are replaced with cysteine, releasing them from the surface of AuNPs and interrupting the FRET process. The high spectral overlap between the blue emission peak of CDs and the absorption spectrum of AuNPs indicates the possibility of this FRET process (Fig. S4). Additionally, the blue emission of CDs can be quenched through reductive electron transfer between CDs and Au3+ ions. Reduction of Au3+ by CDs initiates the nucleation of AuNPs at the surface sites of CDs that provide electrons. This disrupts the electron-hole recombination processes on the CD and transfers electrons in the excited state of CDs to the unfilled orbitals of Au3+, quenching the fluorescence of CDs [46,47].

The fluorescence response of the prepared CDs was further investigated in the presence of potassium permanganate, a common oxidizing agent. As shown in Fig. S5, this oxidant led to a fluorescence response in CDs similar to that induced by Au3+ ions. Therefore, the green emission spectrum of the CDs-AuNPs solution is generated by the oxidation of surface functional groups of CDs, which creates a new emission center in CDs. However, since the blue emission peak of CDs was significantly recovered in the presence of sulfide-containing species, it is suggested that the quenching of the blue emission peak is mostly caused by the energy or electron transfer process to the formed AuNPs, and the surface oxidation pathway is the main factor in the generation of green emission in the presence of Au3+.

To investigate the potential applicability of the nanoprobe in real samples, the determination of Au3+ ions in mineral water samples was investigated. However, no Au3+ was detected in the mineral water sample which was also confirmed by atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAs) with both flame and graphite furnace techniques. Therefore, various concentrations of 0.2, 2, 5, and 10 µM of Au3+ were spiked to the sample and their values were determined using the suggested ratiometric method with the standard addition method. The resulting concentrations and percent recoveries are presented in Table S1. For S2− measurement, known concentrations were spiked into tap water and their values were determined. The resulting values for the spiked concentrations of 0.5, 4, 6, and 8 µM of S2− are summarized in Table S2. The percent recovery values for all spiked measurements (average of five repetitions) were within the satisfactory range of 95 %–105 %, with relative standard deviations lower than 5 %. These results demonstrate the effective applicability of the suggested probe for ratiometric fluorescence measurement of Au3+ and S2− in real samples.

The potential of CDs as fluorescent probes for bioimaging was evaluated in HeLa cells treated with CDs. Firstly, the effect of the synthesized CDs on HeLa cell viability was examined using the MTT assay [48–50]. The MTT assay was elaborately studied with different concentrations of CDs (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, and 500 µg mL−1). As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, 88 % of HeLa cells remained alive even in the presence of a high concentration of CDs (300 µg mL−1) for 24 h, indicating excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of the synthesized CDs. Also, cell viability was estimated to be around 87 % after for 24 h of cultivation with 100 µM Au3+ (Fig. 4b). Therefore, these CDs with low cytotoxicity can serve as effective intracellular nanoprobes for Au3+ [51,52].

The feasibility of using CDs to detect intracellular Au3+ was then studied [7]. Upon laser excitation at 377 nm, the HeLa cells showed blue fluorescence emission from the intracellular region, indicating cellular uptake of CDs (Fig. 5 c). Fig. 5a, d discloses the bright field images of the HeLa cells incubated with CDs and CDs-Au3+ that reveal the normal morphology of the cells in the presence of CDs. To study the intracellular imaging of Au3+, CDs-treated cells were incubated with 10 µM Au3+. Fig. 5 reveals a clear intracellular quenching of blue fluorescence by the addition of Au3+, and as expected, the detection of the green intracellular fluorescence emission upon exciting at 469 nm (Fig. 5f). This indicates that the synthesized CDs can be used for intracellular imaging of Au3+.

Unlike individual PPD which does not show any fluorescence signal under oxidation polymerization, it was demonstrated that a simple hydrothermal treatment of PPD in the presence of sulfuric acid provides a blue-emitting CDs that can reduce Au3+ into AuNPs and simultaneously generate a green emission under oxidation to provide an intrinsic ratiometric fluorescence signal for the determination of trace levels of Au3+. The CDs-AuNPs hybrid probe also showed the possibility of determining cysteine and sulfite through fluorescence ratiometric signals. This feature was almost similar to the fluorescence generated from the oxidation of OPD in the presence of different oxidants, which was observed from PPD after their conversion into CDs. However, the development of OPD-based fluorescence sensors faces a major challenge due to their easy oxidation by various oxidants, such as Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ ions. The synthesized CDs prepared from PPD showed no response to these weaker oxidants indicating their potential to improve anti-interference properties. This is a significant issue, especially in the presence of coexisting oxidants commonly found in real samples. Moreover, it is worth noting that all OPD-based fluorescence probes are single-intensity-based, requiring a second reference fluorophore to provide a ratiometric fluorescence signal. However, the prepared CDs offer an intrinsic ratiometric signal for Au3+ sensing. CDs also exhibit no important cytotoxicity on living cells and showed acceptable potential for intracellular imaging.

Sara Karami: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mojtaba Shamsipur: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ali Barati: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Soheila Mohammadi: Methodology, Investigation. Zahra Abedi Kichi: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124724.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
